data.title: 3 Market Segments I'm Targeting When Iran War Ends
data.description:
Thesis

The conflict with Iran has pushed oil prices above $100 per barrel, with some spikes going even higher in recent days. Rising fuel costs have weighed most on airlines and travel.

Meanwhile, oil and gas firms have held up better.

But conflicts in the Middle East (or elsewhere) tend to follow a pattern: initial escalation, fear, and oil price spike. Then either there's de-escalation (diplomacy, ceasefires), or stabilization, and it's no longer the front page headlines. Initially, markets price in the worst-case scenario early. There are quite a few examples ranging from the Gulf War, Iraq War, and, most recently, the Russia-Ukraine War. I covered this “this too shall pass” scenario in a recent piece about Royal Caribbean (RCL) here.

When the conflict with Iran eventually ends, it could quickly lift a big weight off the global economy.

My core thesis is that the biggest winners will likely be smaller U.S. companies and businesses that rely on consumer spending. And the cleanest way to capture the peace dividend is through the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) for broad small-cap leverage, the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR (XLY) for the spending rebound, and the U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS) for the fastest margin expansion from collapsing fuel costs.

A Simple Bet on U.S. Small Companies

Small companies didn't waste any time getting hit. These businesses usually operate with tight budgets. So when fuel and energy costs go up, they can’t easily pass those extra costs on to customers.

Because of that, the Russell 2000 Index (which tracks small U.S. companies) began to decline. The iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM), which follows that index, also dropped. On the main days when tensions increased, it fell by about 2.15%, and over the past month, it has declined by roughly 6%, as U.S.-focused companies took the impact. That’s exactly why I think IWM is one of the cleanest ways to play a peace dividend.

At its simplest, you’re putting your money into a large group of small U.S. companies, specifically, the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000 Index. The fund is managed by BlackRock (BLK), and the approach is straightforward, just like it sounds. Own the whole index, full stop.

The annual fee (expense ratio) is 0.19%, and the AUM is about $71 billion.

Around 19% is in industrial companies, 17% in healthcare, 16% in financial companies, 14% in technology, and about 9% in consumer-related businesses.

It’s very diversified, and the top 10 holdings make up only about 19% of the total fund, so no single company dominates. The fund keeps it basic. It doesn’t use leverage or complex financial instruments, like derivatives. It just owns stocks. The downside is that it can be quite volatile.

Keep in mind, the fund moves more aggressively than the broader market, with ~23% annual swings.

But the same factors that made IWM vulnerable also explain why it could rebound just as quickly, since IWM reflects the real, everyday activity of the U.S. economy more directly than global companies do. Once the conflict is over and oil prices go back to normal levels, the cost of energy, such as fuel and transportation, will decrease, which should quickly bring overall expenses down. That, in turn, can increase profits for the industrial and financial companies inside IWM, and it can happen fairly quickly. Lower gasoline prices mean people spend less on fuel, so they have more money left in their household budgets. And the sooner, the better; with spring here and summer fast approaching, that extra money often gets spent on things like shopping (retail), which feeds straight into higher demand for the companies in the index.

A Targeted Bet on Consumer Spending

The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) targets companies that rely on consumers having extra spending money. It reacted to the same fears that impacted IWM: higher fuel costs leave households with less to spend, while pricier shipping will feed into higher prices across the board.

Because of this, XLY dropped almost at the same pace as IWM, with only a slight edge in performance. Basically, investors started shifting money, betting people would slam the brakes on shopping, travel, and anything optional, really.

State Street manages the fund to track the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector Index, using full replication of S&P 500 large-cap stocks. It has a low 0.08% expense ratio, about $21.7B in assets, and rebalances quarterly with roughly 10% annual turnover.

The portfolio's (almost) completely made up of consumer cyclical stocks, about 99.9% of it.

My main concern here is that it’s too concentrated. Just two companies take up a very large portion of the total. Amazon (AMZN) makes up about 21% of the portfolio, while Tesla (TSLA) accounts for nearly 19%. That said, I currently rate AMZN as a strong buy.

Looking at the biggest positions, the rest of the top ten holdings include Home Depot (HD), McDonald’s (MCD), TJX Companies (TJX), Lowe’s (LOW), Booking Holdings (BKNG), Starbucks (SBUX), O’Reilly Automotive (ORLY), and Royal Caribbean (RCL), together make up close to 69% of the total portfolio. With the portfolio only holding 52 stocks in total, changes in AMZN and TSLA can have a huge impact on how it performs each day. That said, overall, it's no surprise to see fairly high volatility, at about 23% per year.

Again, like IWM, there’s no use of leverage or complex tools like swaps, it’s just straightforward stock investing. Nice and simple, also a bit more jumpy.

Let's get down to the nitty gritty of the portfolio. XLY is really super sensitive to how consumers feel about the economy. This industry relies on people having extra money to spend on things they don’t really need, items they buy only after covering essential expenses like food, housing, and bills. When oil normalizes, insurance costs fall, and people start saving, fuel first. In turn, that extra savings acts like a small bonus for households, giving them more money to spend on discretionary purchases.

Recent data from the EIA shows that toward the end of last year, as crude oil prices dropped, the average price of gas in the U.S. dropped to ~$2.80 per gallon. This was the lowest level since 2020 when adjusted for inflation. Because of this drop, drivers saved more than $100 per year compared to what they paid in 2024. People then used those savings to spend more in stores, restaurants, and online shopping.

More people started going out to shop. Again, shipping gets cheaper, prices calm down a bit, and families catch a small break. At the same time, people felt better about buying cars. When gas is cheaper, it costs less to own and use a vehicle, so people who had been putting off buying a car felt more comfortable upgrading or even getting a second one. According to Cox Automotive, cheaper fuel helped confidence and kept demand for vehicles on track in the back half of last year.

You can see that travel companies in the XLY sector got an extra boost. Booking Holdings and Royal Caribbean, for example, both made higher profits during past periods when oil prices fell, because fuel for planes and ships makes up a large portion (in the double digits) of their operating costs.

Keep the risks in mind. When the economy slows down, companies that sell discretionary products are usually affected first. However, if things stabilize and energy prices continue to fall, XLY could be one of the simplest and most direct ways to benefit from an economic recovery.

Why JETS Could Rebound Fastest

I think the airlines offer the most dramatic upside potential. The U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS) was one of the sectors hit the hardest during the escalation. Jet fuel costs rose by more than 60% at times, and in some cases even doubled. Since fuel accounts for about 20-30% of an airline’s operating costs, this put a lot of pressure on profits.

As a result, the ETF dropped from recent highs of around $31 to about $24 in the early weeks of the conflict, as profit margins were squeezed.

The fund follows the U.S. Global Jets Index. It includes passenger airlines, aircraft manufacturers, airports, and related services, all selected using a set of predefined rules. It's managed passively by U.S. Global Investors, and the fund has a relatively higher expense ratio of 0.60% with about $764 million in assets.

The top ten holdings make up 62.25% of the fund, with the largest positions being Southwest (LUV) at 13.24%, United (UAL) at 10.29%, Delta (DAL) at 9.99%, and American (AAL) at 8.96%. In total, the fund holds just 57 positions, which means it is fairly concentrated.

Its volatility is quite high, at 37.39% on an annual basis, the highest among the three funds being compared. That high sensitivity cuts both ways. When the conflict ends and oil prices fall quickly, every $1 drop in crude oil goes directly into airline profits. Airlines can keep ticket prices steady or increase their profit margins faster than most other industries.

Look at the chart above, and the pattern is unmistakable (top 4 holding comps; note: Russia/Ukraine ongoing, AAL is the lone underperformer). Every time oil supply gets disrupted, whether by war, geopolitical shock, or even the (hopefully) once-in-my-lifetime travel collapse of Covid, airline stocks get hammered first. Then, once oil stabilizes and uncertainty fades, they have the potential to recover faster than most other groups. That same script could play out with JETS.

Final Takeaway

Ratings: IWM = Buy; XLY = Buy; JETS = Strong Buy. If/when things improve, oil prices and risk premiums could drop quickly, leading to strong gains tied to the economic cycle. Markets have had plenty of time to adjust for a prolonged war. They are not worth reposting as sources, but you might have also come across quite a few posts online and on social media calling for a Covid-like market crash. Since the market’s reaction has been relatively muted, unless things escalate from here, there is reason to believe there is not much additional downside expected. If you can handle volatility, it is a buy across the board. I just would not recommend going "all in" if you are just starting a position in any of these, as the conflict is still ongoing. So if you are conservative or short-term, stay away.

data.title: AC Immune FY 2025 Results: Eyeing ACI-7104 Catalysts
data.description:
Thesis: ACI-7104 to drive upside

AC Immune SA (ACIU) reported a FY25 GAAP EPS loss of CHF 0.70. Again, we saw continued losses typical of a clinical-stage biotech company. However, what was concerning was that revenue came in at just CHF 3.57 million, a hefty 86.9% year-over-year decline, mainly due to the absence of milestone payments they received back in 2024. So overall, the results are very telling of the company’s reliance on partnership-driven income. Overall, I’m quite bullish on AC Immune’s pipeline potential, particularly the progression surrounding ACI-7104 as a first-in-class disease-modifying therapy for early Parkinson’s. The interim Phase 2 data, which have been priced in, were very encouraging, as we saw strong immunogenicity and biomarker engagement. That said, I also think it’s best to wait to see the final Phase 2 Part 1 readout later this year. I expect this to serve as the critical catalyst to reassess upside here for AC Immune.

FY25 results

AC Immune reported FY25 results in mid-March, and on initial viewing, it’s clear that the company is in transition. As you would expect, as they’re moving toward later-stage clinical development, they’re facing the typical financial pressures of a clinical-stage biotech. I think the biggest change is the sharp decline in cash, which fell to CHF 91.4 million from CHF 165.5 million back in 2024. As you know, the reduction is mostly explained by a pretty steep drop in contract revenues, which we saw decrease to just CHF 3.6 million from CHF 27.3 million in the prior year, which we can put down to the absence of a significant milestone payment received in 2024. Now, this goes to show just how much AC Immune relies on partnership-driven income rather than recurring revenues. So in response, the company also implemented some cost-control measures, including about a 30% workforce reduction and a more focused pipeline strategy. As per management, that should allow them to extend their cash runway into 3Q27 despite the lower inflows.

As for the expenses, the company did actually show some discipline. Total operating expenses declined to CHF 72.9 million from CHF 79.7 million in 2024. Specifically, research and development expenses dropped to CHF 56.4 million, from reduced manufacturing and preclinical costs, although we have to mention that this was partially offset by increased spending on newer programs like the NLRP3 inhibitor entering Phase 1 trials. As for general and administrative costs, they also decreased slightly, so overall, there was tighter overhead management.

However, I don’t see these cost reductions as enough to compensate for the significant revenue decline. It resulted in a much higher net loss of CHF 70.5 million compared to CHF 50.9 million in the previous year. And what makes viewing worse is that shareholders’ equity decreased substantially, again indicating ongoing capital consumption. On that note, if we take the total operating expense for FY25, AC Immune has been averaging a cash burn of about CHF 18.2 million per quarter. So if this year's spending stays similar to 2025, we would be looking at an estimated cash runway of about 5 quarters. So if management wants a runway into 3Q27, we need to see much tighter cost control, or else it could be a dilution risk. On that note, the company has guided for expenses to stay much tighter in 2026 at an estimated CHF 55 to CHF 65 million.

Pipeline

Just to give a brief overview of the pipeline, AC Immune is mainly centered on disease-modifying therapies for neurodegenerative disorders, with their lead candidates primarily in Phase 2 development. Now, the most advanced program would be ACI-7104, which is an anti-α-synuclein active immunotherapy for Parkinson’s disease. It’s being designed to stimulate the immune system to clear toxic protein aggregates that we usually see implicated in disease progression. As of now, it’s currently being evaluated in a Phase 2 VacSYn trial with some very encouraging interim data. Elsewhere, ACI-24 targets amyloid-beta in Alzheimer’s disease. Again, this also uses an active immunotherapy approach in which they induce antibodies against pathological proteins. It’s currently in an ongoing Phase 1/2–Phase 2 ABATE trial.

Now, those two candidates would make up the bulk of their pipeline spend going into 2026, but they also have a promising third key asset in ACI-35. This is tipped to target phosphorylated Tau (p-Tau), another hallmark of Alzheimer’s, and is progressing through some mid-stage clinical trials in partnership with Janssen. So the focus here is very much on Parkinson's/Alzheimer’s; however, they do have some lead immunotherapy programs. This would include AC Immune expanding into small-molecule approaches targeting intracellular disease mechanisms. The most notable of which would be ACI-19764, a small NLRP3 inflammasome inhibitor with the goal of reducing neuroinflammation across multiple neurodegenerative and systemic diseases. It has just entered Phase 1 clinical testing.

ACI-7104

So, ACI-7104, which you may have also seen referred to as ACI-7104.056, is an active immunotherapy. Meaning it’s similar to a vaccine-like approach, but with the specific goal of targeting alpha-synuclein (α-syn). We typically see this as the key pathogenic protein that misfolds and aggregates in Parkinson’s disease, so there is definitely a case to go after it. Mechanistically speaking, AC Immune’s thinking here is that they can stimulate the patient’s immune system to produce specific antibodies against toxic oligomeric α-synuclein. So instead of attempting to correct the misfolded protein themselves, they rather stimulate the body to go after it. In theory, if that step works, this should prevent its aggregation and spread between neurons, which is one of the central drivers of neurodegeneration in Parkinson’s.

So the aim here is essentially reducing these toxic protein species, and in doing so, this should interrupt downstream inflammation and neuronal damage, which would set ACI-7104 up as a potential disease-modifying treatment rather than a symptomatic therapy. If we just contrast that quickly to what’s already approved on the Parkinson's drug market, like levodopa or dopamine agonists, these would mainly just address the symptoms but don’t actually alter disease progression.

As for clinical progression, ACI-7104 is currently in a Phase 2 trial (VacSYn) in early-stage Parkinson’s disease. It’s a randomised, placebo-controlled study evaluating safety, immunogenicity, and clinical outcomes. So far, the interim data from Part 1, which included about 34 patients, has been particularly strong for an early-stage neurodegenerative program. The therapy managed to achieve a 100% immunogenic responder rate. They also viewed antibody titers more than 500-fold higher than placebo, which, mechanistically speaking, is a very strong sign.

So there seems to be decent target engagement here. Importantly, AC Immune also recorded multiple biomarkers of disease progression, including CSF α-synuclein, neurofilament light (NfL), and imaging signals, which showed stabilisation. Now, alongside this, they also saw some nice signals of motor function stabilisation as recorded via MDS-UPDRS scores. This would then suggest a potential slowing of disease progression.

As for safety, it has also been quite favourable, with no clinically meaningful adverse events reported yet to date. So based on these results, AC Immune has plans to engage with regulators to define a path toward Phase 3 and potential registration. However, we first need the final Phase 2 Part 1 data, which is expected sometime in mid-2026. I think we should likely see this followed by a larger global study in the major markets such as the U.S. and Europe, if the outcome is positive. The current trial is multinational and focused on early Parkinson’s populations, so it seems the strategy is to intervene before significant neuronal loss.

A word on the TAM

So from a commercial perspective, as you would imagine, a disease-modifying treatment in Parkinson’s disease would have a pretty substantial opportunity. Parkinson’s disease currently affects over 10 million patients globally, with a multi-billion-dollar therapeutic market. Now, the market is still very much dominated by symptomatic treatments. So a truly disease-modifying therapy like ACI-7104 would likely command a premium biologic price, especially since the dosing schedule is set to be vaccine-like, which is very convenient. For some context here, advanced neurodegenerative therapies such as Alzheimer’s antibodies like Leqembi are usually priced at about $26,500 per year in the U.S., so that should give a rough benchmark for potential pricing power in this class. However, it’s worth mentioning that there are currently no approved disease-modifying therapies targeting α-synuclein, but there are several competitors that involve monoclonal antibodies like prasinezumab that are in development. To my knowledge, none have yet demonstrated strong, definitive efficacy. So that leaves the market largely untapped, and ACI-7104, if successful, could capture a significant share of the TAM, particularly in early-stage patients.

Valuation

As you would expect, AC Immune’s valuation is that of a typical clinical-stage biotech, in which limited and highly variable revenues make traditional earnings-based metrics for the most part meaningless. So if we look at the revenue multiples, the stock company currently trades at a pretty elevated trailing EV to sales ratio of 40.6x and a price-to-sales ratio of 65.9x. Both of these multiples sit well above sector medians, and what didn’t help was that big drop in 2025 revenue. So it’s a bit of a distortion, which shows how milestone-driven revenue can temporarily compress valuation multiples, and on the flip side, how their absence has now made the company appear significantly more expensive on a trailing basis.

Instead, I think it’s best to price AC Immune on its cash position and pipeline potential. They hold a market cap of about $293 million and a cash sum of CHF 91.4 million. So a substantial portion of the valuation here would be supported by its balance sheet, with the remaining enterprise value of about $183 million reflecting expectations around future clinical success. I think investors are implicitly assigning some value to upcoming Phase 2 readouts, particularly ACI-7104 in Parkinson’s and ACI-24 in Alzheimer’s. These should serve as the key inflection points moving forward. So in this context, the valuation here would suggest that the market is cautiously pricing in some probability of clinical success, but definitely not a full breakthrough. It leaves a lot of upside contingent on positive trial data.

Risks

It’s worth touching briefly on the risks here, particularly on the uncertainty surrounding ACI-7104. Even with the encouraging interim Phase 2 data and Part 1 of the VacSYn trial showing strong immunogenicity/biomarker improvements, we have to remember that these findings are based on a relatively small dataset. It was also done over a short observation period. So my concern is that in neurodegenerative diseases, early biomarker or clinical signals sometimes fail to translate into durable disease-modifying effects in larger/longer trials. So that leaves a meaningful-sized risk that the final mid-2026 readout may not again confirm the slowing of disease progression. And even if results remain positive, they may not actually be robust enough to shift the standard of care or justify premium pricing.

A second risk I see here would be in the regulatory and development pathway. What we know is that the company intends to use the interim data to engage with regulators and potentially advance ACI-7104 into a pivotal Phase 3 trial. But there is no guarantee that regulatory agencies will agree that the current evidence is sufficient to support such progression. The risk here is that regulators may require additional Phase 2 data, and a much longer follow-up, or alternative endpoints. These, while not fatal to the candidate's trajectory, would definitely introduce a delay to timelines and increase costs.

Looking ahead

As for what’s next, the key focus for investors here should be clinical catalysts, but also their cash management. The most important near-term event is the final Phase 2 Part 1 readout for ACI-7104, expected in mid-2026. This should be critical in validating whether the interim signals can actually translate into meaningful disease modification. Now, other than ACI-7104, we also have the interim data from the ACI-24 ABATE trial in Alzheimer’s sometime in 1H26, as well as early readouts from the ACI-19764 Phase 1 study sometime in the latter half of this year.

So there are a number of upcoming data readouts this year that could all impact the stock. Ultimately, AC Immune’s trajectory over the next 12 to 18 months is going to be driven by whether it can convert the promising early data into late-stage validation. With a key challenge being maintaining sufficient capital to reach those inflection points.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - March 2026
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. The regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. The emphasis here is on high growth rather than high yield. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than in their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook

Since Nov. 2025, the market has been mostly trading in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 7000. There have been so many developments in the last month or so. The issue that is causing the most concern to the market is the ongoing Iran war and the resulting surge in oil prices. This has moved the market closer to SPX6600 rather than SPX7000. There is a lot of uncertainty in the market, and it is difficult to predict how long the Iran conflict is going to last. The longer it drags, the more pain is expected for the energy prices, inflation, and the world economy. This will also likely tie the hands of the Fed, and any further interest-rate cuts may be off the table for now.

On top of that, the other worries, such as AI overvaluations and overborrowing, the fear of the AI impacts on software stocks, and the broader overvaluation of U.S. stocks in comparison with the rest of the world, are still there but have been pushed to the background.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

Note: For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article). This section is common among articles under this series. The regular readers could skip this section.

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth)
  • Payout ratio (dividend per share divided by EPS)
  • Past three years and five-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-year dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/capital and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth during the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 3-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks:

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 3-year dividend growth. This is to account for more recent growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years)

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (3 years)

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with the Highest EPS and RS Rating

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: AU, CCJ, COKE, FIX, LRCX, TRGP, TSM, and WWD (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: HWM (2 duplicates)

Appeared four times: VRT (3 duplicates).

We are now left with 37 (50 - 13) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is one on the list. We remove GE. There are also two others that don't have the sales growth figures readily available, but we will give them the benefit of the doubt and keep them for now. Now, we have 36 names left.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes WIT, AEG, PGR, BSY, and MSFT. We now have 31 (36-5) names.

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. We have no such case. We still have 31 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 60. There are none in our list. However, we remove STRL as it pays no dividends. We are now left with 30 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major:

Finance-Bank-Foreign: We keep CIB but remove BBVA and NWG.

Finance-Regional Banks: We remove BK.

Financial Services and Misc: We keep IBKR but remove CBOE.

Financial- Insurance: We keep CB.

Computer Software:

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH and VRT.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep LRCX but remove TSM.

Consumer-Desc.: We keep TPR, but remove RL and TJX.

Industrial- Aerospace & Defense: We keep HWM and WWD

Industrial- Const. & Engineering: We keep TS.

Industrial- Transportation:

Materials-Gold: We keep AU, but remove KGC and B (Barrick Mining).

Energy-Oil & Gas: We keep CVE and TRGP, but remove PR.

Utility: We keep NFG.

In all, we removed seven names at this stage, so we are finally left with 20 (30-10) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector/segment):

(HWM), (WWD), (FIX), (CIB), (CCJ), (TPR), (VIV), (AXIA), (VRT), (APH), (IBKR), (AU), (CB), (LLY), (CVE), (TRGP), (LRCX), (COKE), (TS), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at our wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is 'not' a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. This month, the turnover is quite low as only two (out of ten) stocks are being replaced.

If a stock from the previous month has been dropped or replaced, it does not mean that the stock is no longer attractive. It simply means that our filtering process has found something more appealing in terms of momentum if two stocks are at par qualitatively.

data.title: Howmet Aerospace: Flying Too High, Some Turbulence To Be Expected
data.description:
Shares of Howmet Aerospace (HWM) have been on a huge momentum run in recent times, with volatility being on the increase as of late. Shares are down 15% from the highs, but continue to trade at real elevated multiples, following strong growth reported here, with Howmet reporting some interesting M&A deals here.

While I like the dealmaking, given the relative valuations and the organic performance, momentum has been too strong to see real appeal. However, I am glad to continue to cover shares here, amidst corporate developments taking place.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including secular growth plays in industrial and aerospace segments, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Taking Advantage Of Stanley's Woes

Towards year end, Howmet announced a $1.8 billion cash deal to acquire Consolidated Aerospace Manufacturing, a designer, and manufacturer of precision fasteners, fluid fittings, and other complex and highly-engineered products for aerospace and defense applications. The deal results in some significant, yet unknown, tax benefits.

The deal is seen contributing about $490 million in sales in 2026, with EBITDA margins seen exceeding 20%; that is before synergies. That suggests that EBITDA is seen around $100 million on a standalone basis, as the effective purchase price drops to 13 times, factoring in unknown costs and tax synergies.

That reveals to me that the deal comes at a price of around 3.6 times sales, about 18 times EBITDA, and about 13 times EBITDA, once tax and cost synergies are accounted for. These are not necessarily dirt-cheap multiples, but in the grand scheme of aerospace valuations, they look relatively compelling.

Adding To An Aerospace Empire

In February, Howmet reported its 2025 results. Revenues rose by 11% to $8.25 billion that year, with the business being very profitable, posting GAAP operating earnings of $2.05 billion for margins equivalent to a quarter of sales. Net earnings of $1.51 billion come in at $3.71 per share on a diluted basis, with adjusted earnings coming in six cents higher, all of that looking quite clean.

That business is well diversified. Engine products make up about half of sales, these being very lucrative sales with EBITDA margins reported at a third of sales. This is accompanied by a $1.7 billion fastening systems segment, these being very lucrative activities as well, with margins reported around 30% of sales. Both engineered structured and forged wheels are about a billion-dollar business with margins reported in the twenties.

Trading around a high of $260 per share, the equity valuation peaked around $104 billion, which, combined with a net debt load of $2.3 billion, makes for a $106 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 13 times sales, making the purchase of Stanley's assets look like a steal, with the recent deal adding about 6% to pro forma sales. Needless to say, from a valuation perspective, shares traded at a very demanding 70 times earnings multiple.

This comes after shares have taken off over the past decade; this was just a $30 stock a decade ago, in fact trading at similar levels as recently as 2022. Shares broke the $100 mark towards year-end 2024, with incredible momentum seen ever since.

The company guided for a continuation of growth, with 2026 sales seen at a midpoint of $9.10 billion, with sales seen up around 10%. Adjusted EBITDA is seen around $2.76 billion, with earnings seen up around a point to just over 30% of sales. All this should result in earnings advancing further to $4.45 per share, marking real leverage, with earnings multiples seen down to 58 times based on the guidance, although that was applied to the peak valuation at $260 per share. Shares have now pulled back to $228 per share, as valuations remain demanding at around 51 times earnings.

From the outlook and conference call, it was not obvious if the guidance included the Consolidated deal, or not, but common practice is that it does not. If that is the case, some real accretion could be seen, at least in terms of sales, but limited immediate earnings accretion in the near term given additional financing costs to be incurred.

Furthermore, the company announced a deal for Brunner, valued at $120-$150 million, adding about $60 million in sales. That adds nearly a percent to overall sales, taking place at a compelling 2.0-2.5 times sales multiple.

With pro forma net debt seen around $4.2 billion, leverage is far from an issue, with EBITDA seen around $2.8 billion based on the guidance for the year and recent M&A contribution. This provides a roadmap for earnings to advance to $5 per share, at least as a run rate by year-end, as even such achievements translate into a 45 times earnings multiple here, while leverage is seen around 1.5 times.

What Now?

The reality is that the valuations are very demanding, as has been the case for a while now. Aerospace at large has been on a massive run recently, yet current events cause some concerns here amidst sky-high valuations. Part of this re-rating has also been driven by a thriving gas turbine unit, with the company being a derivative of the data center boom as well.

However, the company is of real quality, as that is just what has been priced into the stock, with the track record being strong, although aided by the positioning. While the company has seen some real advancements in sales, margins, and a reduction in the share count, the share price advancement has far outpaced these earnings achievements on a per-share basis, making me naturally cautious.

Even with shares down 15% from the absolute highs, shares are seen back to levels last seen as recently as early February, with shares still having doubled over the past year or so. That actually followed shares having doubled in the year before, as an indication of the momentum seen. That is what makes me cautious, despite continued and bolt-on dealmaking at very compelling (relative) valuations here.

This makes me like the relative arbitrage of recent M&A, but the absolute valuations are very demanding, making me cautious here, as much lower valuations should be seen before I am willing to reconsider my stance.

data.title: Unilever: Portfolio Reshaping Advances With Food Business Under Review
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Unilever (UL), I highlighted how their planned ice cream spin-off and 2030 plan could help improve their margins, although there were several macro headwinds to keep in mind.

Although the stock is down to 52-week lows following the escalation of the Iran conflict, broader macro pressure, and recently announcing their intention to look into a potential spin-off/sale of their food business, I believe UL’s current valuation remains fair, with the margin of safety not justifying a buy yet.

Internal Developments

UL reported a relatively solid quarter and year, with a 3.5% growth rate in underlying sales in 2025, 2 percentage points coming from price growth and 1.5 points from volume growth, the latter being quite rare in this environment, being an advantage of the company’s global footprint, while their 30 “Power Brands” (78% of turnover; 11 of them being billion-dollar brands) delivered even better numbers.

Meanwhile, the free cash flow (excluding the Magnum Ice Cream (MICC) branch that was successfully demerged recently - with UL keeping 19.9% of them, for now - reached €5.9 billion, with a 100% cash conversion rate, while they also completed several other portfolio dispositions and bolt-on acquisitions.

For 2026, Unilever expects a similar 4% to 6% underlying sales growth rate, with modest underlying operating margin improvements from last year’s 20% - which remains solid for a company in this industry, further highlighting their diversified, global operations with significant exposure to Asia (44% of their turnover, double the 22% coming from North America) - with CAPEX expected at about 3% of their turnover.

Financially, based on UL’s latest report, we can see an overall normal position for a company like this, with the current assets not covering the current liabilities, but with an overall manageable level of debt, with a relatively low 2.0x Net Debt/Underlying EBITDA, reducing their net debt by €1.4 billion following the demerger.

Unilever also announced a new €1.5 billion share buyback announced after spending €1.5 billion in 2025 as well, while the 3.77% dividend represents about €4.45 billion, for a combined yield of about 5.13% based on the current price and a combined payout of roughly 100% of the FCF. Note that this FCF is down compared to 2024 mostly due to costs associated with the Magnum demerger, meaning that their “real” cash flow would’ve been enough to support this yield.

Potential Food Spin-Off

Based on recent reports, Unilever is considering another spin-off following the Magnum one that was recently completed, this time for their legacy food business (about a quarter of turnaround, expected to be dethroned by personal care and beauty in the medium-term), although it’s still exploring ideas right now and we can’t know if it will be a full split or just a select number of brands - if any.

However, although a public spin-off may not happen immediately, the company already announced talks in this regard from companies like Kraft Heinz (KHC) and McCormick (MKC) - recently confirmed by Unilever in a press release - which can potentially result in some deal even this year given how much interest was seen in just a few days.

Although it’s early to conclude anything, this move is not the worst, as it can allow the company’s strong brands to shine in more concentrated entities instead of trading them at a “conglomerate discount,” separating the higher-growth beauty and personal care (which can also be bought by a giant in their respective industries if they want to sell everything) from the slower and mature food business.

However, this move also raises M&A-specific risks to keep in mind (e.g. eliminating synergies, weaker bargaining power), which likely had an impact on the market given the drop that came with the initial announcement. Not only that, but selling anything during an arguably weak environment can risk undervaluing it, as sales/cash flow/margins/etc. data that would go into determining the value of these assets today have been under significant pressure from the broader environment, potentially losing some value for the shareholders.

Far From The Best Macro Setting

Although we’re talking about a diversified, global giant, it’s important to note that the current environment is far from great, with significant issues and trade-offs/trade-downs reported by companies from virtually every industry. This is a normal effect of a high-rate environment, but the macro pressure from tariffs and now the Iran conflict added extra layers of uncertainty and stress. This geographic advantage was also highlighted by Unilever’s CFO during their Q4 Earnings Call, stating:

We delivered balanced growth across developed and emerging markets despite a more uneven macro and consumer backdrop through the year. This highlights the advantage of our geographic footprint. In developed markets, we grew ahead of our categories despite consumer conditions softening, particularly in the second half. In emerging markets, performance improved throughout the year, reflecting decisive actions we took to address challenges alongside improved execution, a step-up in innovation and a more focused channel execution as well as an improving trading environment in several key markets.

In fact, the latter can lead to significantly worse than just some consumer weakness, as it already has visible effects on fuel prices, with oil recently reaching nearly $120/bbl again following the recent escalations, which, alongside the rise in gas and fertilizer prices (since the region is key for these as well) can be the final drop for an already weak global economy, potentially leading to a recession.

Even though this company may seem relatively defensive, the ongoing trade-offs and pressure seen in recent years can prove quite the opposite, as even the products that are considered defensive can come in lower price or better value forms.

For this, retailers and their white labels are a big fundamental threat that can be boosted significantly by these trends, which not only limits the available shelf space and thus hurts the legacy brands’ power of negotiation, but it also generates immense amounts of valuable data. Once things improve, retailers such as Walmart (WMT), Kroger (KR), etc. can then offer better price points that would directly compete with the likes of Unilever, especially since they’d be able to capitalize on the “free” advertising they got for their private brands, with consumers trusting them even more (which is a very important aspect for the purchasing decision).

Once again, even though they are a global producer with strong brands, these long-term trends can cause fundamental disruptions, although it’s more likely to see companies with weaker brands that are easier to replace be affected more, at least at first.

Valuation

For UL’s FCF to Equity valuation, starting from their $6.844 billion in 2025 (converting everything into USD for this one), which I'll assume can grow to $7.5 billion in 2026 to take into account a reversal from this year’s taxes combined with their growth potential, without accounting for any change in net borrowings to highlight their pure cash flow generation potential. Note that I won’t account for any further spin-offs yet since it’s currently just an idea, and there’s no certainty that anything will happen, so far.

I’ll assume this can grow at a CAGR of 6% over the first 5 years, which is in line with their expected net sales growth rate, possible thanks to their portfolio reshaping initiatives on top of potential macro improvements and Magnum stake sellout to fund other initiatives, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and 2.75% terminal growth rate (slightly better thanks to their significant exposure to the higher-growth Asian market), with a discount rate of 10% to take into account the competition, macro trends and solid financials, as well as 2.18 billion shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $117.73 billion, for an intrinsic value of $54 per share, which is below the current $61.47, while slightly better assumptions get us pretty much at today’s levels.

Conclusions

I reiterate UL’s Hold rating, as the valuation seems fair now and the macro environment has plenty of room to deteriorate, while broader macro trends and pressure can turn out to be significant headwinds for legacy brands, which is why I’ll continue to demand a better margin of safety especially since there are better options in today’s market for growth, value, dividends, or all three.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Digital Realty Trust: The Reasons I Am Staying On The Sidelines
data.description:
Owning an equity REIT, like Digital Realty Trust (DLR), could be lucrative to many investors who want to have exposure to the real estate market but do not want to commit to owning a property directly. It is even more lucrative, when the REIT is active in the data center space, which is currently experiencing significant secular tailwinds due to the huge CAPEX spending on AI infrastructure buildout. Last, but not least, DLR's steadily growing dividends over the past two decades could also be attractive for income-focused investors, especially as the firm's income stream is relatively independent from consumer sentiment, meaning that the dividends are not likely to be in danger, even if people stop spending on discretionary goods.

Like with every investment, an investment in DLR also comes with risks. First, DLR is heavily reliant on debt, meaning that if the interest rates were to stay elevated for longer, their cost of capital could also increase, putting downward pressure on the bottom line results. Also, the price we pay for the stock, significantly impacts our return potential for the future. I believe now DLR is not valued attractively enough to start a new position or to add to an existing one, based on a dividend discount model.

I started covering DLR in August 2023, with my last article having been published over a year ago in February 2025. Each time I wrote about the firm, valuation was raising concerns, despite the attractiveness of the business as a whole.

1. DLR is an attractive data center REIT

DLR is an attractive REIT right now as the demand for their property is strong, driven by the AI infrastructure build out ambitions. If you want to enter the real estate market, but you do not want to commit to buying a physical property, starting with investing in a REIT could be a viable option. And DLR's presence in the data center space is especially lucrative. They also have a large, diversified portfolio of properties, consisting of more than 300 data centers in more than 55 metro areas, serving over 5500 customers.

Relying on a large number of tenants over different geographic locations makes DLR less dependent on a single client or single revenue stream, making their business more robust. If any of the tenants were to default or if any of the geographic regions were to experience weaker demand, DLR could still survive and thrive elsewhere. And when I talk about different geographic locations, I am not only talking about the United States, but about the entire globe.

When it comes to growth, the firm plans to keep growing globally, expecting more than 5 GWs of future development capacity across the globe.

Further, the company's strong backlog also demonstrates the high demand for DLR's services, and also allows us to gauge how the rent revenue is likely to develop in the coming years. A total backlog of $1.4 billion is definitely a good sign.

All in all, I believe that DLR has a solid, well-diversified, growing business, supported by secular tailwinds. This is, however, not the full story. While one might think that all these justify a buy rating, there is much more to it - namely valuation and debt.

2. Valuation and Dividends

To estimate DLR's fair value, I am going to be using a dividend discount model today. Why is this model suitable? DLR has been a reliable dividend payer for more than two decades, and over this period they also managed to increase the payments significantly. REITs are actually obliged to pay out at least 90% of their annual taxable income to the shareholders as dividends to maintain their REIT status. Currently, the firm pays a quarterly dividend of $1.22 per share, equivalent to an annual yield of roughly 2.8%.

When it comes to dividend discount models, there are two key input parameters: required rate of return and dividend growth. For the required rate of return, I normally use the firm's cost of equity, which is estimated to be 7.34% currently for DLR.

For the dividend growth rate, I generally tend to use longer-term historic averages. In DLR's case it might not be a good idea. Their 10-year dividend CAGR is 3.59%, which would be a very aggressive assumption to use in perpetuity, especially when we consider that in the past years the growth significantly slowed.

For my first case, I will assume 0% dividend growth in perpetuity. This results in a fair value of $71 per share, which is less than 50% of the current market price. One might say that these assumptions may be too pessimistic, however.

So let us look at a more realistic case, and let us assume that DLR's dividend growth rate is going to be similar to the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy - 2.5% in perpetuity. In this case, the company's fair value would come in around $111 per share, still roughly 37% lower than the current share price.

So what is the stock really priced for, then? Even if I assume that the secular tailwinds due to the AI infrastructure buildout are going to benefit DLR's business significantly in the coming years, and assume a 10% dividend growth rate in the coming 5 years and 2.5% afterward in perpetuity, the fair value still comes in at $154 per share, more than 10% below the current market price.

If you believe, however, that sustained double-digit growth is feasible in the next couple of years, then it may be a buying opportunity for you. If the firm actually manages to achieve such high growth rates, it would not only allow them to increase the dividends at a higher pace, but it would also possibly warrant a multiple expansion, which could lead to a significant upside potential. This scenario may not be entirely impossible, as the firm's guidance for fiscal 2026 also assumes double digit growth across many metrics. The question is, how long the tailwinds are going to last, and how long DLR will be able to benefit from it at this rate. At this point, this thesis has too many uncertainties for my taste.

Last but not least, the fact that insiders have been only selling in the past years also does not convince me that the stock is attractively priced right now.

I believe that DLR is not attractive from a valuation point of view, considering the most recent dividend growth trends, and the stock price should significantly come down, before it could start becoming attractive for value focussed investors.

3. Debt

With the ongoing geopolitical tension in the Middle East, debt is starting to become a larger problem. The issue is primarily driven by the elevated energy prices. High-energy prices often lead to inflation, as the cost of transportation and manufacturing increases. Higher inflation means that the Fed is not likely to cut interest rates in order to tame inflation.

Higher Fed funds rates lead to higher borrowing costs and therefore higher cost of capital. DLR has significant debt in its capital structure and therefore high interest rates directly increase the risk of the investment.

While DLR is trying to match the duration of its assets and liabilities, in 2027 and 2028, they will have $4.9 billion maturing, a significant portion of it in USD. If interest rates were to remain elevated, the refinancing cost of these debts could be also high. How interest rates will develop, will be very much dependent on how long the war in the Middle East lasts.

The high oil price is not only problematic because of its potential impact on inflation and interest rates, but also because operating data centers is a very energy-intensive business. Higher energy prices higher operating costs lead to a significant negative impact on the tenants' financial performance.

Conclusions

While DLR's business appears attractive at first sight due to its large, diversified portfolio and the secular tailwinds provided by the significant spendings on AI infrastructure build out, there is much more to it.

DLR appears to be significantly overvalued according to my dividend discount models, leaving little upside potential from the current price levels. The insider selling activity also leaves little room for optimism.

The firm's high reliance on debt could also be problematic, interest rates were to remain higher for longer, as a significant portion of DLR's debt is maturing in 2027 and 2028. High energy prices could also put downward pressure on the tenants' financial performance as data centers are very energy intensive.

All in all, I do not think it is attractive to start a new position in DLR now, or to add to an existing one.

data.title: Aveanna Healthcare Holdings: Recent Results Have Me More Optimistic (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis of Aveanna Healthcare Holdings (AVAH). When I last wrote about the company in July of 2025, I rated its stock as a Hold citing tight margins and potential challenges ahead from government payment for healthcare services. Fast forward to today and AVAH has gained a whopping 60.6% in the last seven and a half months since that article was published on Seeking Alpha. The source for most of these gains in the stock came after a quarterly report on August 7 buoyed AVAH over 50% in the next two trading days. On August 11, the company's rating was bumped up to Outperform by Raymond James. On September 11, it earned an upgrade from Underweight to Neutral from JP Morgan.

Aveanna Healthcare has experienced a very different market reaction to its Q4 2025 results, which were released on March 19. Its stock fell over 10% on the news even though the company reported GAAP earnings from the quarter of $0.80 per share, $0.65 better than expectations. The drag on the stock price came from the company’s projections for next year that included a disappointing revenue outlook. A graph that shows the performance of AVAH over the last 12 months is shown below.

What should be made from the last year’s roller coaster ride for AVAH? Did the market overreact when the stock rose significantly in August and September? Or are potential buyers being too pessimistic now? When everything is put together over the last 12 months, Aveanna Healthcare ended up with a total return that was just a few percentage points ahead of the S&P 500, once dividends are included.

Based on the most recent financial data available for the company, I am a now more optimistic for AVAH's future than I was last July. Aveanna Healthcare has made some noticeable improvements in its overall profitability, and it is growing in healthcare markets that are likely to continue to see high demand in the year ahead. At a time when so many other stocks are being impacted by higher energy prices and possible slowdown in economic growth, the company's home healthcare services should be largely unaffected by those concerns and provide some diversification from other, more cyclical investments. I am raising my assessment of the stock to a Buy, but this is not a rating that comes without significant risk, as I will discuss in my analysis below.

Company Overview

Aveanna Healthcare Holdings is a provider of a range of home-based healthcare services. The company cares for patients and families in 38 states through its network of over 360 office locations. The bulk of its operations take place in the Southeast and along the East Coast. A map of the company’s service area with additional information about its operations can be seen below.

Aveanna Healthcare Holdings separates its operations into three divisions when reporting its financial results. Those three are as follows: Private Duty Services, Home Health and Hospice, and Medical Solutions. Private duty services, or PDS, is by far the most important for the company from a revenue perspective. It is this business segment that offers skilled private nursing, non-clinical support, and pediatric therapy. In FY 2025, PDS provided over 80% of the total revenue for the company. A breakdown of the revenue contribution from each segment along with their gross margins is given below.
